content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum


January 27, 1983


TO:     File

FROM:   Barb[illegible]/man/Ted Prim

RE:     Gifts to State for Use by Governor Deukmejian,
        Our File No. M-83-006


        Vance Ray, counsel to the Governor, contacted the Commission
staff for advice on the Act's disclosure and disqualification
requirements in connection with receipt of a limousine and
furnishings for the Governor's mansion from private donors.
Members of the Legal Division met with the Executive Director to
analyze the question under the Stone opinion, 3 PPPC 52, and
concluded that the Stone opinion was distinguishable from the
case at hand in several respects.

        First, unlike the Stone opinion, the key issue here is
disclosure since the disqualification requirement contained in
Government Code Section 87100 has very little effect on the
Governor. This is because there is no remedy for violations of
the disqualification provision by elected state officers.

        Second, unlike the air transportation in Stone, the items
being given for Governor Deukmejian's use are objects which
generally will continue to have value to the State after
Governor Deukmejian's term of office. If we insist they are
personal gifts to Governor Deukmejian, he could logically take
them with him when he leaves office. We certainly do not wish
to encourage that result.

        Finally, treating these personal gifts to Governor
Deukmejian is probably contrary to the purpose of the gifts,
which seem to be in a tradition of gifts given to state
buildings, public museums, the White House, or governors'
mansions. Such gifts are generally understood to be gifts to
the government, although they may be temporarily used or enjoyed
by government officials.

P/. ··

Memo to File
January 27, 1983
Page Two


For these reasons, we concluded that gifts of furnishings
for the Governor's mansion, the gift of a limousine for
transportation of the Governor and other high level executive
officials and similar gifts were gifts to the State of
California rather than to Governor Deukmejian personally.
Accordingly, we advised the Governor's Office that receipt of
these gifts need not be reported on Governor Deukmejian's
Statement of Economic Interests if:

1.  The State has title to the gifts;

2.  The State exercises substantial control over the use of
the gifts;

3.  The gifts will not become the personal property of
Governor Deukmejian after his term of office; and

4.  The Governor's Office, the Department of Finance or some
other designated agent notifies the Commission when such gifts
are received.  This notification should describe the gift,
provide identification of the donors, and give the estimated
value of the gifts.  The notification is a public record and
will be available for public inspection at the Commission
offices.

BAM/TP:km

sections.conclusion: Gifts of furnishings for the Governor's mansion, a limousine for transportation, and similar gifts are gifts to the State of California rather than to Governor Deukmejian personally, and need not be reported on the Governor's Statement of Economic Interests if: (1) The State has title to the gifts; (2) The State exercises substantial control over the use of the gifts; (3) The gifts will not become the personal property of Governor Deukmejian after his term of office; and (4) The Governor's Office, Department of Finance, or designated agent notifies the Commission when such gifts are received with a description, donor identification, and estimated value.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether gifts of a limousine, mansion furnishings, and similar items provided by private donors to the Governor must be disclosed on the Governor's Statement of Economic Interests under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that gifts to the Governor for official use (such as a limousine and mansion furnishings) are gifts to the State rather than personal gifts, and therefore need not be reported on the Governor's personal disclosure statement if four conditions are met: State ownership and control, non-personal disposition after the Governor's term, and Commission notification. The FPPC distinguished this case from the Stone opinion based on the permanent value these items retain for the State.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is heavily redacted/damaged with significant text illegibility, particularly in the first page. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers are present; extracted text from narrative content. The Stone opinion reference and four-part test are clearly articulated. Confidence is moderate due to document quality issues but core legal analysis is discernible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether gifts of a limousine, mansion furnishings, and similar items provided by private donors to the Governor must be disclosed on the Governor's Statement of Economic Interests under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: Gifts of furnishings for the Governor's mansion, a limousine for transportation, and similar gifts are gifts to the State of California rather than to Governor Deukmejian personally, and need not be reported on the Governor's Statement of Economic Interests if: (1) The State has title to the gifts; (2) The State exercises substantial control over the use of the gifts; (3) The gifts will not become the personal property of Governor Deukmejian after his term of office; and (4) The Governor's Office, Department of Finance, or designated agent notifies the Commission when such gifts are received with a description, donor identification, and estimated value.

Members of the Legal Division met with the Executive Director to analyze the question under the Stone opinion, 3 PPPC 52, and concluded that the Stone opinion was distinguishable from the case at hand in several respects. First, unlike the Stone opinion, the key issue here is disclosure since the disqualification requirement contained in Government Code Section 87100 has very little effect on the Governor. If we insist they are personal gifts to Governor Deukmejian, he could logically take them with him when he leaves office. We certainly do not wish to encourage that result. Accordingly, we advised the Governor's Office that receipt of these gifts need not be reported on Governor Deukmejian's Statement of Economic Interests if: 1. The State has title to the gifts; 2. The State exercises substantial control over the use of the gifts; 3. The gifts will not become the personal property of Governor Deukmejian after his term of office; and 4. embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter addresses whether gifts of a limousine and mansion furnishings provided by private donors to Governor Deukmejian must be disclosed on his Statement of Economic Interests. The FPPC concluded these are gifts to the State rather than personal gifts and set forth four conditions under which they need not be reported.
